From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Track and field event

Not to be confused with [Long jump](/wiki/Long_jump "Long jump").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/1912_Benjamin_Adams.JPG/250px-1912_Benjamin_Adams.JPG)](/wiki/File:1912_Benjamin_Adams.JPG)

Benjamin Adams during the standing long jump competition at the 1912 Summer Olympics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/1912_Gustaf_Malmsten.JPG/250px-1912_Gustaf_Malmsten.JPG)](/wiki/File:1912_Gustaf_Malmsten.JPG)

Gustaf Malmsten during the standing long jump competition at the 1912 Summer Olympics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/1912_Konstantinos_Tsiklitiras3.JPG/250px-1912_Konstantinos_Tsiklitiras3.JPG)](/wiki/File:1912_Konstantinos_Tsiklitiras3.JPG)

Konstantinos Tsiklitiras during the standing long jump competition at the 1912 Summer Olympics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/1912_Platt_Adams4.JPG/250px-1912_Platt_Adams4.JPG)](/wiki/File:1912_Platt_Adams4.JPG)

Platt Adams during the standing long jump competition at the 1912 Summer Olympics

The **standing long jump**, also known as the **standing broad jump**, is an [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)") event. It was an [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") event until 1912. It is one of three standing variants of [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") jumping events, which also include the [standing high jump](/wiki/Standing_high_jump "Standing high jump") and [standing triple jump](/wiki/Standing_triple_jump "Standing triple jump").

In performing the standing [long jump](/wiki/Long_jump "Long jump"), the jumper stands at a line marked on the ground with the feet slightly apart. The athlete takes off and lands using both feet, swinging the arms and bending the knees to provide forward drive. The jump must be repeated if the athlete falls back or takes a step at take-off.

In the early part of the nineteenth century the standing long jump was a popular event at [highland games](/wiki/Highland_games "Highland games") and military sports, but very few of these performances were measured. On Friday 8 July 1853 the [93rd Sutherland Highlanders](/wiki/93rd_(Sutherland_Highlanders)_Regiment_of_Foot "93rd (Sutherland Highlanders) Regiment of Foot") held their annual Highland Games on [Chobham Common](/wiki/Chobham_Common "Chobham Common") at which Private J. Forbes cleared 9ft 3in (2.82m) in the standing long jump.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

John Scriven of [Cambridge University](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") also cleared 9ft 3in (2.82m) at the [St John's College](/wiki/St_John%27s_College,_Cambridge "St John's College, Cambridge") Sports at [Fenner's](/wiki/Fenner%27s "Fenner's") cricket ground on Thursday 15 March 1859.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Less than a week later, on the first day of the Cambridge University Sports on Monday 21 March, William Howard, of [Trinity College](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge"), Cambridge, cleared 10ft 2in (3.10m).[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

At the annual regimental sports of the [24th Regiment](/wiki/South_Wales_Borderers "South Wales Borderers"), at [Shorncliff Camp](/wiki/Shorncliffe_Army_Camp "Shorncliffe Army Camp"), near [Cheriton](/wiki/Cheriton,_Kent "Cheriton, Kent") in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), on Thursday 16 September 1864, three men cleared ten feet. Private Lynch cleared 10ft 6in (3.20m), Private Mullis 10ft 3in (3.12m), and Private Crosey 10ft 1in (3.07m).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[Huddersfield](/wiki/Huddersfield "Huddersfield") Athletic Club held their first Annual Athletics Festival at the Rifle Ground, Trinity Street, Huddersfield, on Saturday 24 June 1865, at which two men, W. N. Haigh and J. W. Rhodes, tied at 10ft 8in (3.25m).[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Two months later, on Friday 18 August 1865, at [Duns](/wiki/Duns,_Scottish_Borders "Duns, Scottish Borders"), in the borders of Scotland, around fifteen miles west of [Berwick-upon-Tweed](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed"), at a meeting of Duns Athletic Club, W. Allen cleared 10ft 9in (3.27m) in the standing long jump and 32ft 2in (9.80m) in the standing triple jump.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Eleven feet was first cleared less than a week later. On Saturday 14 August 1865, Private Rushton (H Troop) cleared exactly 11ft 0in (3.35m) at [Aldershot](/wiki/Aldershot_Garrison "Aldershot Garrison") during the Regimental Sports of the [14th Hussars](/wiki/14th_King%27s_Hussars "14th King's Hussars").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Ten months later, on Saturday 24 June 1866, two men cleared eleven feet. At the second Annual Athletic Festival of Huddersfield Athletic Club both M. H. Bradley and J. W. Rhodes cleared 11ft 0in (3.35m) at the Rifle Ground, Trinity Street,
Huddersfield.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Exactly one year after that, at the third annual festival of Huddersfield Athletic Club on Saturday 13 July 1867, J. Duckworth, of [Haslingden](/wiki/Haslingden "Haslingden") Athletic Club, cleared 11ft 3 1/2in (3.44m).[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

At [Carrick-on-Suir](/wiki/Carrick-on-Suir "Carrick-on-Suir") in [County Tipperary](/wiki/County_Tipperary "County Tipperary") in southern [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), fifteen miles north west of [Waterford](/wiki/Waterford "Waterford"), on Monday 1 April 1872, [Maurice Davin](/wiki/Maurice_Davin "Maurice Davin"), a farmer who later became the first President of the [Gaelic Athletic Association](/wiki/Gaelic_Athletic_Association "Gaelic Athletic Association") (GAA), cleared 11ft 6in (3.50m). He also won the 56lb [weight throw](/wiki/Weight_throw "Weight throw") at 26ft 4in (8.02m), the [shot put](/wiki/Shot_put "Shot put") (17lb) with 38ft 8in (11.78m), the [hammer](/wiki/Hammer_throw "Hammer throw") (16lb) 98ft 0in (29.88m), the [standing triple jump](/wiki/Standing_triple_jump "Standing triple jump") 32ft 7in (9.93m), and had a walkover in the [high jump](/wiki/High_jump "High jump").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

On Saturday 13 May 1876, at the [Chuckery Ground](/wiki/The_Chuckery "The Chuckery"), [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall "Walsall"), during the Second Annual Athletic Sports of [Walsall Cricket & Football Club](/wiki/Walsall_Cricket_Club "Walsall Cricket Club"), A. B. Lindop jumped 11ft 10in (3.60m).[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[Ray Ewry](/wiki/Ray_Ewry "Ray Ewry") set the first world record for the standing long jump at 3.47 m (11 ft 4+1⁄2 in) on September 3, 1904. The current unofficial record is held by [Byron Jones](/wiki/Byron_Jones_(American_football) "Byron Jones (American football)"), who recorded a jump of 3.73 m (12 ft 2+3⁄4 in) at the [NFL Combine](/wiki/NFL_Combine "NFL Combine") on February 23, 2015,[[18]](#cite_note-18) beating the official world-record jump distance of 3.71 m (12 ft 2 in) set by Norwegian shot putter Arne Tvervaag from Ringerike FIK Sportclub in 1968, in a different setting with different controls.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

France held national championships at standing long jump and standing high jump for men from 1905 to 1920, and for women from 1917 to 1926. Their first standing long jump champions were Henri Jardin (Racing Club de France) 3.04m (9ft 11 3/4in), and Suzanne Liebrard (Fémina Sports) 2.215m (7ft 3in).[[20]](#cite_note-20)

When indoor arenas were built, the standing long jump began to disappear as an event. Today, [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") is the only country where the standing long jump is a national championship event. The Norwegian Championships in Standing Jumps (long jump and [high jump](/wiki/Standing_high_jump "Standing high jump")) has been held at the [Norwegian Indoor Athletics Championships](/wiki/Norwegian_Indoor_Athletics_Championships "Norwegian Indoor Athletics Championships") every winter since 1995.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The standing long jump is also one of the events at the [NFL Combine](/wiki/NFL_Combine "NFL Combine"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) it was one of the standardized test events as part of the [President's Award on Physical Fitness](/wiki/President%27s_Council_on_Fitness,_Sports,_and_Nutrition#Awards "President's Council on Fitness, Sports, and Nutrition"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) as well as the physical fitness test that officer cadets must complete at the [Royal Military College of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_of_Canada "Royal Military College of Canada") and the [United States Air Force Academy](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force_Academy "United States Air Force Academy").[[25]](#cite_note-25) In the Brazilian police forces, a minimum performance in a standing long jump test is required to join the Federal Police (2.14 m for men 1.66 m for women[[26]](#cite_note-26)) and the Federal Highway Police (2.00 m for men 1.60 m for women).[[27]](#cite_note-27)
